mission.title: Install and Configure Fluid on Kubernetes
mission.description: Fluid is an open source Kubernetes-native Distributed Dataset Orchestrator and Accelerator designed for data-intensive applications, such as big data and AI applications. Installing Fluid allows you to efficiently manage and accelerate data workflows in cloud environments.

steps.title: Add the Fluid Helm repository
steps.description:
First, add the Fluid Helm repository to your Helm client using the following command:
```bash
helm repo add fluid https://charts.fluid.io
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Fluid using Helm
steps.description:
Now, install Fluid with the following command, specifying the desired namespace (e.g., fluid-system):
```bash
helm install fluid fluid/fluid --namespace fluid-system --create-namespace --version v1.0.8
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check if the Fluid pods are running successfully with:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace fluid-system
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
To ensure optimal performance, configure resource limits for the Fluid components. Edit the values.yaml file or use the --set flag during installation. For example:
```bash
helm upgrade fluid fluid/fluid --namespace fluid-system --set resources.limits.cpu=500m --set resources.limits.memory=512Mi
```

steps.title: Set up monitoring (optional)
steps.description:
For monitoring Fluid, you can integrate it with Prometheus. Ensure that the metrics collection is enabled in the configuration:
```bash
helm upgrade fluid fluid/fluid --namespace fluid-system --set properties.alluxio.user.metrics.collection.enabled=true
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Fluid will show the Fluid pods running in the specified namespace. You can verify this by running 'kubectl get pods --namespace fluid-system'. Additionally, resource limits should be configured as per your requirements.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add fluid https://charts.fluid.io
helm repo update
helm install fluid fluid/fluid --namespace fluid-system --create-namespace --version v1.0.8
helm upgrade fluid fluid/fluid --namespace fluid-system --set resources.limits.cpu=500m --set resources.limits.memory=512Mi

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
To cleanly remove the Fluid installation, use the following command to uninstall the Helm release from the specified namespace:
```bash
helm uninstall fluid --namespace fluid-system
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
After uninstalling the Helm release, you should clean up any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims (PVCs) that were created. Use the following commands:
```bash
kubectl delete crd datasets.fluid.io
kubectl delete crd datamaps.fluid.io
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace fluid-system
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Finally, remove the namespace used for Fluid and verify that it has been deleted:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace fluid-system
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, it's a good practice to back up the current state of your Fluid installation. You can export the current configuration with:
```bash
helm get values fluid --namespace fluid-system > fluid-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository to ensure you have the latest charts, then run the upgrade command to update Fluid to the desired version:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade fluid fluid/fluid --namespace fluid-system --version v1.0.9
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After upgrading, verify that the Fluid pods are running the new version successfully:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace fluid-system
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If you see that the Fluid pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, you can diagnose the issue by checking the logs:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace fluid-system
``` 
Look for any error messages that may indicate what is causing the crash.

troubleshooting.title: Failed to pull image
troubleshooting.description:
If the pods are failing due to image pull errors, verify that the image name is correct and that your cluster has access to the image repository. Check the events for the pod:
```bash
kubectl describe pod <pod-name> --namespace fluid-system
```

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient resources
troubleshooting.description:
If the pods are not starting due to insufficient resources, check the resource requests and limits set for the pods. You can view the pod specifications with:
```bash
kubectl get pod <pod-name> -o yaml --namespace fluid-system
``` 
Adjust the resource limits as necessary.

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access the Fluid services, ensure that the services are running and check their endpoints:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace fluid-system
kubectl describe svc <service-name> --namespace fluid-system
``` 
Make sure that the service type is set correctly (e.g., ClusterIP, NodePort) for your use case.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and both Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
